2 min read
Just now

--

https://franksmithlll.com/images/article-covers/frank-smith-iii-readme-documentation-likeness-cover-2026-07-21.jpg
A full-stack project is more than code. It is also the explanation around the code: what the project does, why it exists, how to run it, what is finished, and what still needs work.
That is why I have been paying closer attention to README files. I am a Fullstack Academy graduate, a full-stack developer, and a Field Operations Specialist working in water treatment in Bergen County, New Jersey. The field operations side of my work taught me that notes and procedures matter because real systems depend on clear handoffs.
Software projects need the same discipline.
When someone opens a repository, I want the README to answer the first practical questions: What does this application do? What is the current status? What technologies are actually used? How does someone run it locally? What workflow is being demonstrated? What limitations should a reviewer know about?
The README should not exaggerate the project. It should make the project easier to understand.
For projects like Cutz By Casper, Jukebox Pro, Book Buddy, Sturgis Options, and the Isaac Wright Jr. Advocacy Website in development, documentation helps keep the public explanation accurate. It also gives recruiters and collaborators a clearer path into the work.
A good README usually includes a few basics:
Project purpose. A short explanation of what the application does and who it is for.
Current status. Whether the project is live, deployed, in development, a prototype, coursework, or archived.
Verified stack. The technologies actually used, explained by responsibility instead of listed as keywords.
Setup steps. Install commands, local run commands, and environment-variable guidance without exposing secrets.
Workflow notes. A short walkthrough of how the main user path works.
Screenshots. Images that show the real interface without private information.
Limitations. Honest notes about what needs more polish, testing, or expansion.
Next improvements. A practical view of where the project can go next.
I see documentation as part of the build. It is not separate from the technical work. If I build something and cannot explain it clearly, then part of the work is still unfinished.
I expanded this into a full technical article on my portfolio:
https://franksmithlll.com/building-better-readme-files-full-stack-projects-frank-smith-iii
